Patient is supine. The affected leg is placed in flexion, abduction, and external rotation (figure-4 position). Examiner applies gentle downward pressure on the knee.
Sign is positive if pain is reproduced in the groin or there is restricted movement.
If positive, indicates hip joint pathology such as OA.
Patient is supine. Examiner flexes the hip to 90°, then adducts and internally rotates the hip.
Sign is positive if groin pain is reproduced.
If positive, indicates intra-articular pathology (OA or FAI).
Patient is supine. Examiner flexes and adducts the hip, then applies axial compression while moving the hip in an arc.
Sign is positive if pain, grinding, or crepitus is felt.
If positive, suggests hip joint degeneration (OA).
Patient is supine with legs extended. Examiner gently rolls the leg internally and externally.
Sign is positive if pain or increased external rotation is noted.
If positive, indicates intra-articular hip pathology.
Patient stands on one leg. Examiner observes pelvic alignment.
Sign is positive if pelvis drops on the opposite side.
If positive, indicates gluteus medius weakness associated with hip OA.

Patient stands on the affected leg for up to 30 seconds.
Sign is positive if lateral hip pain is reproduced within 30 seconds.
If positive, indicates gluteal tendinopathy associated with GTPS.
Patient lies supine with hip flexed to 90°. Examiner passively externally rotates the hip, then asks patient to return leg to neutral against resistance.
Sign is positive if pain is reproduced over the lateral hip.
If positive, suggests gluteus medius/minimus tendinopathy (GTPS).
Patient lies on side with unaffected leg down. Examiner extends and abducts the affected hip, then allows it to drop into adduction.
Sign is positive if the leg remains abducted and does not drop.
If positive, indicates ITB tightness contributing to GTPS.
Patient stands on one leg while examiner observes pelvic alignment.
Sign is positive if pelvis drops on the contralateral side.
If positive, indicates gluteal weakness associated with GTPS.

Patient is supine. Examiner flexes the hip to 90°, then adducts and internally rotates the hip.
Sign is positive if groin pain is reproduced.
If positive, indicates femoroacetabular impingement or intra-articular pathology.
Patient is supine. The leg is placed in flexion, abduction, and external rotation (figure-4 position). Examiner applies downward pressure on the knee.
Sign is positive if pain is reproduced or there is asymmetry compared to the other side.
If positive, suggests hip joint pathology such as FAI.
Patient is supine. Examiner flexes and adducts the hip, then applies axial compression while moving the hip through an arc.
Sign is positive if pain, clicking, or grinding is reproduced.
If positive, indicates intra-articular pathology such as FAI or labral involvement.
Patient is supine with hip flexed to 90°. Examiner applies internal rotation with overpressure.
Sign is positive if groin pain or restriction is noted.
If positive, suggests FAI due to limited internal rotation.

Patient is supine. Examiner flexes, adducts, and internally rotates the hip.
Sign is positive if groin pain or clicking is reproduced.
If positive, indicates labral pathology or FAI.
Patient is supine. Examiner applies axial load while moving the hip in a circular motion.
Sign is positive if pain, clicking, or catching is felt.
If positive, suggests labral tear or intra-articular pathology.
Patient is supine with legs extended. Examiner gently rolls the leg internally and externally.
Sign is positive if increased external rotation or pain is observed.
If positive, indicates intra-articular pathology such as a labral tear.
Patient lies supine and pulls one knee to chest while the other leg hangs off the table.
Sign is positive if the hanging leg produces hip pain or clicking.
If positive, may indicate labral irritation or associated hip pathology.

Sign is positive if pain is reproduced in neutral rotation.
If positive, indicates early intra-articular pathology (AVN suspicion).

If positive, indicates intra-articular pathology (use red flags to suspect AVN).

Sign is positive if snapping is reproduced.
If positive, confirms snapping hip syndrome.
Patient lies on side. Examiner extends and abducts the hip, then allows it to drop.
Sign is positive if the leg remains abducted.

Sign is positive if snapping or pain occurs in anterior hip.
